channel.title: Hiking Log on Keenan Ganz
channel.description: Recent content in Hiking Log on Keenan Ganz

item.title: Lake Serene
item.description:
6/2/2019

Lake Serene is the Mt. Si of the Highway 2 corridor. That is to say - strenuous yet beautiful hiking and lots of people. My first experience with the trail was a line of some 20+ signs saying “NO PARKING” on the side of the road. After parking in what was probably the last spot in the lot (at like 7 am!!) and starting up the trail, I was soon walking behind a group playing music out of a speaker.

So yeah… good start.

If people are a problem for you, save this one for the off season and bring spikes. But if you can deal with a conga line, Lake Serene is probably the most spectacular hike on Highway 2. Nearby Bridal Veil Falls feeds the lower sections of the trail with quaint ponds and creeks. Absolutely no issues with the trail, probably owing to the maintenance warranted by the crowds.

Roughly half way up the trail you come to a turn off for Bridal Veil Falls. Take this on your way up; you will be rewarded with a torrential waterfall paired with your first good view of the ridgelines on the north side of the highway.

The trail becomes a little more difficult as you get closer to the lake. But press on and reach the crown jewel of this trail: over a square mile of open water framed by scree and snowmelt.

No overnight use is permitted, but as you get closer to the lake there are plenty of small clearings to use as lunch spots. The water is pure snowmelt and a welcome top-off for what you drank on the way up. As an added bonus, the difference in elevation from the lake to the ridge top on the south side of the lake is roughly the same as the elevation you just climbed on the trail!

Swimmers are a common site, but do take care to practice leave no trace. When I visited, there were plenty of people being disrespectful of other users (see: playing music, dropping beer cans, etc.). One would hope that the lake will remain in pristine conditions for future generations of hikers.

In short: maybe save this one for the fall. And, bring a trash bag to clean up what others may have left behind.

item.title: Mailbox Peak
item.description:
08/24/2020

The fact that anyone still hikes the god-forsaken thing that is the old Mailbox Peak trail is absolutely baffling to me. Let me give you some numbers. The nearby Mt. Si trail is a reasonably strenuous training hike. Over a four-mile route, that trail ascends some 3,150 feet. For those keeping score, that’s an average grade of 18.5%, or a little less than 1 foot of elevation gain for every 5 feet traversed. The old Mailbox Peak trail packs a heavier punch at 4,000 feet of ascension. But, here’s the kicker, it does so in only 2.7 miles. Grade: 28.0%.

To my knowledge, this is the only day-hike on the I-90 corridor that was rebuilt not because of storm damage or forest management, but because people kept getting hurt on the darn thing. Today, the far more reasonable route tackles the elevation gain over 10 miles. However, the old path occasionally cuts across the present trail as a stark reminder of Mailbox’s gluttony for punishment.

Despite its hobbling, Mailbox packs a punch worthy of the early drive to secure a parking spot. There are only a few flat sections to give brief respite from the constant plodding uphill. Don’t reserve water breaks for “the next switchback”; they can be spaced out as far as a mile. And, don’t mistake a break in the trees for victory. Upon rising out of the tree line to a field of talus, you’ll be met with arguably the most difficult section that climbs 700 feet over a half mile. If you took the new trail, this is what you were missing from the old.

While trying to ignore the lactic acid in your legs, take a moment to recognize the fantastic trail work that went into building stairs through the rocky section out of individual boulders. We’re lucky to have the WTA and its volunteers; my ankles would like to personally thank them.

Following the rocks and a short scamper up a thinly-vegetated stretch, we come to the pièce de résistance: the mailboxes on top of Mailbox. Yes, you read right, there are now two of the things up there. In addition to the true mailbox, with its commemorative plaque and fine crust of graffiti, is a wimpy appendix on the eastern side of the summit. Don’t miss the DNR datum from 1983 a few steps away from the main attraction. Many have written about the wide array of items one can find stashed in the mailbox. Some highlights from my visit include:

  • Biden campaign mail
  • A full bottle of Fireball whiskey
  • A cool pen
  • Mysterious Amazon Prime packages

The Fantasy Costco vibe wears off pretty quickly when you realize that most of the stuff people leave in the mailbox is pretty much a step or two above trash. The WTA claims that there is a summit registry, but I didn’t see the wisdom in digging around a cursed public receptacle during a pandemic for the sake of bravado.

On the way back down, I couldn’t help but feel a little underwhelmed by the reality of the legend. Perhaps the new trail takes away from Mailbox’s identity, demoting it to 1.5x Mt. Si a few exits past North Bend. Can one really call Mailbox an adventure if there’s a bus stop and a ready-made rock staircase just below its summit? At the very least, more can share in the joy of some of the finest views in the Snoqualmie valley. And, maybe the legend truly lives in the hearts of the hikers who keep coming back for more.

item.title: Mt. Si
item.description:
12/31/2019 (most recently)

One cannot recreate in Washington without eventually going to Mt. Si. The trail is well-maintained, connects to other long-distance trails, and you can literally take a bus to it from Seattle. I haven’t personally taken that route, but dang would it be cool to get to a hike via Orca card.

In fact, taking the bus is probably the optimal way to get to Mt. Si. The hike’s extreme popularity means that the parking lot, already quite large, fills up in the morning during the peak season. Get there as early as you can (or, like myself, go in the off season).

Anyway, I like to use Si to open the hiking season. The route is the perfect mix of rugged switchbacks and familiarity that makes missing gear less severe while still busting your calves. No wonder this is the most popular hike in the state.

For first-timers, make sure you take some time to look at the interpretive signs roughly a mile into the hike. Mt. Si has some interesting mythology in local Native American cultures and to this day you can still see remnants of wildfires early in the 20th century.

As for sights, the way up is mostly your standard PNW second-growth forest with occasional exposed rock faces. Mt. Si was designated as a state conservation area only in 1977. Before that, mining and logging tore out all of the original scenery.

Proceeding upward, the cedars and douglas firs transition to thinner mountain hemlock forest with occasional peaks through the trees. The first real view point is above some scree next to a heavily-vandalized trail marker. Continue through a short shaded trail before reaching the true destination of the hike: Haystack Rock.

Scrambling up the back side of Haystack is doable (as those who stand atop it can atest), but in my opinion a sketchy scramble isn’t the best way to cap off a hike of Si’s intensity. There’s plenty of space for lunch and pictures, just be careful of squirrels and birds who make a living on hikers’ refuse (remember: no feeding!).

Personally, I’ve been to Si enough that I don’t even take pictures anymore. It’s way more fun taking pictures for people who are summiting for the first time. In summary: get up early for parking, enjoy the switchbacks, and soak in the perfect starter hike on the I-90 corridor.

item.title: Mt. Washington
item.description:
7/5/2020

Fun story about this one. With the pandemic still in full swing, masks are an essential part of any form of outdoor recreation. So, you can imagine how silly I felt when after 10 minutes of driving to Mt. Washington, I realized that I had left my mask at home. Or, so I thought. After making the drive back and opening my trunk, there the mask was.

I felt a little sullen over wasting a half hour in transit. Today, after all, was fourth of July weekend and I had made the somewhat foolhardy decision of picking a popular hike. These feelings were perhaps compounded by the fact that I then got lost in the entrance to Olallie state park while looking for the Mt. Washington trailhead.

To anyone else planning on hiking Mt. Washington, here’s the way to the trailhead in as simple of instructions as I can provide. On I-90 going east, take exit 38. and go right at the T-intersection. Then, take the next right immediately. The WTA page says you will cross a river before finding the turnoff. This is true, but you can’t really see the river from the road (or, maybe I’m just blind). Continue down this dirt road to the Homestead Valley Trailhead. Despite the name difference, this is the right spot.

Start on the trail, going rapidly uphill. You will come to the wide, graded, gravel-paved Palouse to Cascades trail (fun fact: this trail continually traverses almost the entire state of Washington!). Go right (west) and look for an intersection with a narrow footpath on the left (south) side. This trail should be marked for Mt. Washington.

Now, confident that you are in fact not lost, begin your walk upward. As you traverse the gradual uphill grade, look out for cool rock faces on the upslope side of the trail. Climbing is popular in the area, with pitons marking out the most common routes.

The trail continues upward before opening up somewhat at a ridge saddle. The trail makes a sharp bend coincident with a different trail, but the route then turns straight up the final ridge to the peak of Mt. Washington. As you near the summit, be sure to check behind you for good views of Diablo Lake. The summit itself is somewhat small, so consider taking lunch somewhat earlier.

When I reached the summit (thankfully I was a few minutes ahead of the crowds), the morning clouds had not yet burned off. But just a few moments before I got up to leave, they parted enough for me to get this picture!

Maybe it was a good thing I thought I forgot my mask :)

Have fun, and stay safe out there!
